question:
A round-robin tournament of $2n$ teams lasted for $2n-1$ days, as follows.
On each day, every team played one game against another team, with one team winning
and one team losing in each of the $n$ games. Over the course of the tournament,
each team played every other team exactly once. Can one necessarily choose
one winning team from each day without choosing any team more than once?
solution:
days with $1\leq m\leq 2n-1$, there are at least $m$ distinct teams that
won a game on at least one of those days. If not, then any of the teams
that lost games on all of those days must in particular have lost to $m$
other teams, a contradiction.

If we now construct a bipartite graph whose vertices are the $2n$ teams
and the $2n-1$ days, with an edge linking a day to a team if that team
won their game on that day, then any collection of $m$ days is connected
to a total of at least $m$ teams. It follows from Hall's Marriage Theorem
that one can match the $2n-1$ days with $2n-1$ distinct teams that won on
their respective days, as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
A round-robin tournament of $2\halfteams$ teams lasted for $2\halfteams-1$ days, as follows.
On each day, every team played one game against another team, with one team winning
and one team losing in each of the $\halfteams$ games. Over the course of the tournament,
each team played every other team exactly once. Can one necessarily choose
one winning team from each day without choosing any team more than once?
descriptive_long.solution:
days with $1\leq \daycount\leq 2\halfteams-1$, there are at least $\daycount$ distinct teams that
won a game on at least one of those days. If not, then any of the teams
that lost games on all of those days must in particular have lost to $\daycount$
other teams, a contradiction.

If we now construct a bipartite graph whose vertices are the $2\halfteams$ teams
and the $2\halfteams-1$ days, with an edge linking a day to a team if that team
won their game on that day, then any collection of $\daycount$ days is connected
to a total of at least $\daycount$ teams. It follows from Hall's Marriage Theorem
that one can match the $2\halfteams-1$ days with $2\halfteams-1$ distinct teams that won on
their respective days, as desired.

days with $1\leq ftzlnhgw\leq 2kqxsdrpt-1$, there are at least $ftzlnhgw$ distinct teams that

kernel_variant.question:
A double round-robin tournament is held among \(2n \;(n\ge 2)\) teams.
The schedule is fixed in the customary way  

*  on the first \(2n-1\) consecutive calendar days every unordered pair
   of teams meets exactly once and every team plays exactly one match
   per day;  

*  during the following \(2n-1\) days the same list of pairings is
   repeated in the same order, but with the home/away roles
   interchanged.  

Hence the competition lasts \(4n-2\) days and on every day \(n\) matches
are played, producing \(n\) (decisive) winners.
For every day \(d\in\{1,\dots ,4n-2\}\) let \(W(d)\) denote the set of
the \(n\) winners of that day.

Prove that one can always choose a single winner  

\[
f(d)\in W(d)\qquad(d=1,\dots ,4n-2)
\]

so that

(i)  every team is selected on at most two different days, and  

(ii) if a team is selected twice, the two selections occur in different
     halves of the schedule, i.e. one in \(\{1,\dots ,2n-1\}\) and the
     other in \(\{2n,\dots ,4n-2\}\).

Thus a team may be distinguished twice, but never twice ``too soon''; the
two distinctions must lie in the two opposite legs of the tournament.
kernel_variant.solution:
1.  A capacitated bipartite model  
    Give each real team two formal copies  

      \(T^{\sf F}\)  (first half)  and  \(T^{\sf S}\)  (second half).

    Build the bipartite graph  

    \[
      G=(\mathcal D,\mathcal T;\,E),\qquad
      \mathcal D=\{1,2,\dots ,4n-2\},\;
      \mathcal T=\{T^{\sf F},T^{\sf S}\mid T\text{ a team}\}.
    \]

    For every day \(d\) and for every winner \(T\in W(d)\) join  
    \(d\) to \(T^{\sf F}\) if \(1\le d\le 2n-1\) and to \(T^{\sf S}\)
    if \(2n\le d\le 4n-2\).

    *  In \(G\) we shall look for a matching that covers
       the whole left class \(\mathcal D\).
       A matched edge \((d,T^{\sf F})\) (or \((d,T^{\sf S})\))
       tells us to put \(f(d)=T\).  

    *  Because neither \(T^{\sf F}\) nor \(T^{\sf S}\) can be used
       twice in a matching, condition (ii) is automatic, and the real
       team \(T\) is selected at most twice, guaranteeing (i).

    Hall's marriage theorem therefore finishes the problem once we have  
    proved that every subset of days has at least as many neighbours:

    \[
       \forall\,\mathcal X\subseteq\mathcal D\;:\;
       |\Gamma(\mathcal X)|\ge|\mathcal X|.
       \tag{H}
    \]

    Because the two halves of the schedule are completely
    independent, it suffices to establish (H) separately
    for the first and for the second half.  We treat the first half;
    the second is identical.

2.  Hall's condition inside one half  
    Let \(\mathcal X\subseteq\{1,\dots ,2n-1\}\) consist of
    \(m:=|\mathcal X|\ge 1\) first-half days and set  

    \[
        \mathcal W:=\bigcup_{d\in\mathcal X} W(d)
    \quad(\text{the teams that win on at least one of those }m\text{ days}).
    \]

    We claim that  
    \[
        |\mathcal W|\;\ge\;m. \tag{*}
    \]
    Then \(|\Gamma(\mathcal X)|=|\mathcal W|\ge m=|\mathcal X|\),
    proving (H).

    Proof of (*).  
    Suppose, to the contrary, that \(|\mathcal W|=k<m\).
    Because \(k<2n\), there exists at least one team
    \(U\notin\mathcal W\); that team never wins on the chosen
    \(m\) days and therefore \emph{loses on each of them}.

    On every one of the \(m\) days the opponent who beats \(U\)
    necessarily belongs to \(\mathcal W\); call the opponent of
    day \(d\) by \(V_d\).
    As the first half is a single round robin, \(U\) meets each rival at
    most once, so the opponents \(V_d\;(d\in\mathcal X)\) are pairwise
    distinct.  Consequently  

    \[
       |\mathcal W|\;\ge\;\bigl|\{V_d\mid d\in\mathcal X\}\bigr|
       \;=\;m,
    \]
    contradicting the assumption \(k<m\).  Hence (*) is true.

3.  Finishing      \
    By the discussion in step 1 the first-half inequality (*) (and its
    verbatim counterpart for the second half) is precisely what Hall's
    theorem requires.  Therefore \(G\) contains a matching of size
    \(|\mathcal D|=4n-2\).

    Orient every matched edge away from the day vertex and put
    \(f(d)=T\) whenever the edge is \((d,T^{\sf F})\) or \((d,T^{\sf S})\).
    Because the matching uses each vertex \(T^{\sf F},T^{\sf S}\) at
    most once, the map \(f\) satisfies conditions (i) and (ii),
    completing the proof. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Extra structural layer – two legs with home/away reversal – doubles the number of days and introduces “sweep pairs’’ (linked days that must behave coherently).  

2.  The selection problem now involves _capacities_ (each team may appear twice) and _coupled slots_ (the two appearances must come from the same sweep pair), so ordinary Hall’s Theorem is no longer sufficient; one needs its _capacitated_ version or an equivalent integral-flow formulation.  

3.  Establishing Hall’s condition is subtler: one must show that every set of m days collectively touches enough _capacity_, not merely enough vertices.  The proof demands a refined counting argument (Step 3) instead of the single-line estimate that sufficed in the original problem.  

4.  Turning the capacitated instance into an un-capacitated matching via vertex-splitting and invoking the integrality of flows are techniques that lie beyond the elementary graph theory used in the original solution.  

Altogether the problem intertwines matchings with capacity‐and‐dependency constraints, requires creating an auxiliary flow network, and calls for a more intricate counting lemma, making it significantly harder than both the original problem and the currently existing kernel variant.
